FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, COVID-19 Rapid Response Research Release May 2020, n=5412, U.S. national sample aged 10-14, 21 recruitment sites, NIH-funded.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using multiple linear regression to estimate associations between mental health/resiliency factors and total screen use among 5412 ABCD participants.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed letter in JAMA Pediatrics (2022); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Table 2 reports regression coefficients for mental health, perceived stress, social support, and coping behaviors predicting TOTAL screen time. No per-modality regression coefficients for phone/SM-specific variables (texting, social media, video chatting) against any individual-trait outcome are found anywhere in the paper. Step 2: The total screen time composite includes streaming/movies (2.42 h/d), multiplayer gaming (1.44 h/d), single-player gaming (1.17 h/d), social media (0.98 h/d), texting (0.84 h/d), video chatting (0.65 h/d), and browsing the internet (0.42 h/d). This composite bundles TV/streaming and gaming (non-phone/non-SM items) with phone/SM items — NOT exclusively phone/SM ingredients — with no separate per-modality breakout in the regression analyses. FT-IC2 NOT_MET.

notes: Total screen time composite includes streaming, single-player gaming, multiplayer gaming, texting, social media, video chatting, and internet browsing. The regression results pair this composite (not phone/SM-specific) with individual-trait outcomes (mental health, perceived stress, social support, coping). No per-modality regression estimates for phone/SM-specific variables are reported.

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, COVID-19 survey data Release 3.0, May 2020, N=5335, U.S. national sample ages 10-14, 21 sites, NIH-funded.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using paired t-tests, intraclass correlation coefficients, and multiple linear regression to compare parent- and adolescent-reported recreational screen time and assess sociodemographic predictors of discrepancy scores.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Academic Pediatrics (2022); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Table 4 reports regression coefficients (B) for sociodemographic predictors (age, sex, race/ethnicity, country of birth, primary language, parent marital status, parent education, household income) predicting parent-adolescent discrepancy scores in total recreational screen time, multiplayer gaming, texting, social media, and video chatting. Texting, social media, and video chatting are qualifying phone/SM-specific variables with their own per-modality coefficients. However, the other side of ALL analyses consists exclusively of sociodemographic variables (age, sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parental education, marital status, country of birth, primary language). Per the criteria, if the only analyses pair the SM/phone variable with demographic-only on the other side, FT-IC2 = NOT_MET. No analysis pairs a phone/SM variable with an individual-trait-level outcome (mental health, behavioral, cognitive, etc.). NOT_MET.

notes: The paper's central focus is on parent-adolescent discrepancies in screen time reporting. All inferential analyses (Table 4) regress sociodemographic characteristics onto phone/SM-specific screen time discrepancy scores. The other side of every analysis is purely demographic; no individual-trait-level variable (health, behavioral, cognitive, etc.) appears on either side of any regression.

FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional data from the ABCD Study Year 2 follow-up (2018-2020), 4.0 release, N=9429, U.S. national sample predominantly 11-12 years old, 21 recruitment sites, NIH-funded.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using multiple logistic regression to estimate associations between sociodemographic factors and cyberbullying victimization/perpetration, and between screen time modalities and cyberbullying outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Academic Pediatrics (2022); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Table 3 reports adjusted OR estimates for specific phone/SM-specific screen time modalities (texting: AOR 1.15, 95% CI 1.09-1.22, p<.001; social media: AOR 1.21, 95% CI 1.15-1.27, p<.001; video chat: AOR 1.16, 95% CI 1.09-1.23, p<.001) predicting cyberbullying victimization. Social media (AOR 1.19, 95% CI 1.12-1.27, p<.001) and texting (AOR 1.15, 95% CI 1.06-1.24, p=.001) also predict cyberbullying perpetration. These are qualifying phone/SM-specific variables with individual coefficients. The other side — cyberbullying victimization and perpetration — is a behavioral outcome (within-person behavioral construct) qualifying as individual-trait-level. MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: Adjusted OR estimates with 95% CIs reported for social media, texting, and video chat predicting cyberbullying victimization and perpetration in Table 3 (e.g., social media AOR 1.21, 95% CI 1.15-1.27 for victimization).

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple logistic regression analyses with quantitative OR estimates, 95% CIs, and p-values reported for phone/SM-specific variables predicting cyberbullying outcomes in Table 3.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional; condition (a) of FT-EC7 (longitudinal design) is not met. FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: Primary analyses (Table 2) examine sociodemographic predictors of cyberbullying — cyberbullying alone would not qualify IC2, but Table 3 provides separate per-modality screen time regressions with social media, texting, and video chat as qualifying IVs predicting cyberbullying as a behavioral DV. INCLUDE.
